learn.title: Electric Vehicles: Powering the Future
learn.intro: Electric vehicles (EVs) are revolutionizing transportation by reducing emissions and dependence on fossil fuels. Understanding how they work is key to embracing a cleaner, more sustainable future.
learn.c1_title: Electric Motors: The Heart of EVs 🔧
learn.c1_text: EVs use electric motors powered by batteries to convert electricity into motion. Unlike internal combustion engines, motors provide instant torque, enabling quick acceleration. Most EVs use AC induction or permanent magnet motors for efficiency.
learn.c2_text: Regenerative braking captures kinetic energy during deceleration and converts it back into electricity, extending range. This system can recover up to 70% of braking energy, significantly improving efficiency compared to traditional friction brakes.
learn.c3_title: EV Charging Levels: From Home to Highway 🔌
learn.c3_text: Level 1 (120V) adds 3-5 miles per hour, Level 2 (240V) offers 10-20 miles per hour, and DC fast charging can add 60-100 miles in 20 minutes. In India, public charging infrastructure is expanding rapidly, with over 20,000 charging points installed as of 2023.
learn.stat2: Global EV sales: 10 million units in 2022
learn.stat4: India's FAME scheme: Target of 30% EV penetration by 2030

learn.title: Perovskite Solar Cells: Next-Gen Efficiency
learn.intro: Perovskite solar cells promise higher efficiency and lower costs than traditional silicon panels. Their rapid development is a game-changer for solar energy adoption globally.
learn.c1_text: Perovskites have a unique crystal structure that absorbs light efficiently. Their bandgap can be tuned to capture more sunlight, potentially reaching 30% efficiency, compared to 22% for standard silicon panels.
learn.c2_title: Tandem Cells: Stacking for More Power 🔋
learn.c2_text: Perovskite-silicon tandem cells combine two layers to capture more of the solar spectrum. This setup has achieved over 33% efficiency in labs, doubling the output of single-junction cells.
learn.c3_text: India aims to install 500 GW of renewable energy by 2030. Perovskite tech could reduce costs and boost efficiency, aligning with the National Solar Mission's goals. Research initiatives are underway to commercialize this technology.

learn.title: EV Charging Infrastructure: Levels Explained
learn.intro: Understanding EV charging levels is essential for planning long trips and home charging. Each level offers different speeds and use cases, impacting convenience and accessibility.
learn.c1_text: Level 1 uses a standard 120V outlet, adding 3-5 miles of range per hour. It's ideal for overnight charging at home but not suitable for fast top-ups. Most EVs come with a Level 1 charger.
learn.c2_text: Level 2 uses 240V and adds 10-20 miles per hour. It's commonly found in public stations and homes. In India, Level 2 chargers are being deployed in cities to support urban EV adoption.
learn.c3_text: DC fast chargers deliver 60-100 miles in 20 minutes. They're crucial for long-distance travel. India is expanding its DC fast charging network, with 5,000+ stations planned by 2025.
learn.stat2: DC fast charging: 150 kW average power
learn.stat4: India's FAME II: Target 100,000 EV charging stations by 2025

learn.title: Vehicle-to-Grid (V2G): Powering the Future
learn.intro: V2G technology allows EVs to send power back to the grid, stabilizing energy supply and reducing costs. This innovation is transforming how we manage electricity demand and storage.
learn.c1_title: V2G Basics: EVs as Energy Storage 🔋
learn.c1_text: V2G enables EVs to discharge stored energy back to the grid during peak demand. This helps balance supply and demand, reducing the need for fossil fuel-based peaker plants. In India, pilot projects are testing V2G in urban areas.
learn.c2_text: By aggregating EV batteries, V2G systems can provide frequency regulation and voltage support. This reduces grid stress and enhances reliability. Studies show V2G can cut grid costs by up to 30% in urban areas.
learn.c3_title: India's V2G Potential: A Grid Game-Changer 🇮🇳
learn.c3_text: India's grid faces challenges with renewable integration. V2G could help manage variability from solar and wind. The government is exploring policies to incentivize V2G, with trials in cities like Delhi and Mumbai.
learn.stat1: India: 100 MW V2G capacity planned by 2025
learn.stat2: V2G can reduce grid costs by 25%
learn.stat4: V2G pilot projects: 5 cities (Delhi, Mumbai, etc.)

learn.title: Floating Offshore Wind: Deep Water Innovation
learn.intro: Floating offshore wind platforms unlock vast untapped wind resources in deep waters. This technology is critical for expanding renewable energy in regions with limited shallow-water sites.
learn.c1_text: Floating wind turbines use mooring systems to stay stable in deep water. They can operate in depths over 150 meters, accessing stronger winds. In the US, the Wind Energy Technologies Office is funding R&D for this tech.
learn.c2_text: Mooring systems like tension leg platforms and semi-submersibles ensure stability. These systems reduce motion and improve turbine efficiency. The US aims to deploy 15 GW of floating wind by 2035, leveraging this tech.
learn.c3_title: India's Offshore Wind: Deep Water Potential 🇮🇳
learn.c3_text: India's offshore wind potential is 100 GW, but shallow sites are limited. Floating platforms could unlock deep-water resources. The National Institute of Wind Energy is researching this tech for India's coastal regions.
learn.stat2: US floating wind target: 15 GW by 2035

learn.title: Wind Resource Assessment: Finding the Best Sites
learn.intro: Wind resource assessment identifies optimal locations for wind farms by analyzing wind patterns. This process is vital for maximizing energy output and minimizing costs.
learn.c1_text: Anemometers measure wind speed and direction at various heights. Data from these instruments helps model wind patterns. In India, wind resource assessments have identified the Western Ghats as a high-potential region.
learn.c2_text: Wind atlases use historical data and models to map wind speeds. They guide developers to the best sites. India's Wind Resource Atlas highlights the Deccan Plateau as a key area for wind energy development.
learn.c3_title: India's Wind Potential: A National Priority 🇮🇳
learn.c3_text: India has a wind potential of 100 GW, with 60% in the western regions. The government's National Wind Energy Mission targets 100 GW by 2022. Wind resource assessments are critical for achieving this goal.
learn.stat2: Western Ghats wind speed: 7-8 m/s (annual average)
learn.stat4: National Wind Energy Mission: 100 GW target by 2022
